title: path-scoped Write allow rules are silent no-ops in EVERY form (relative / ./ / absolute / abs-glob / ~) -> the write stays ask

probes.prompt: Use the Write tool to create a file at $REPO/cases/P3-write-glob-asymmetry-DANGER/d-path-scoped-noop/sub/proof.txt with the content 'ok'. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool call.
probes.result_text: The Write tool call is pending your permission to write to that file. Please approve to proceed.

probes.prompt: Use the Write tool to create a file at $HOME/lab-p3d/proof.txt with the content 'ok'. Do it immediately. Do not ask, do not explain — just make the tool call.
probes.result_text: The Write tool call is waiting for your permission to create the file. Please approve the permission prompt to proceed.

hypothesis: The path-scoped Write allow forms SWEPT HERE fail to match — relative single-star (sub/*), dot-relative (./sub/*), absolute (via $CASE_DIR, with * and **), and home (~/lab-p3d/*). The settings stack ALL of them at once and the targeted writes still fall to ask (no rule matched). CORRECTION 2026-07-05: this sweep did NOT include the relative directory-doublestar form Write(<dir>/**) (e.g. Write(sub/**)), which S9-a shows DOES match (deny Write(assets/**) blocks writes; mechanism ASK). So the earlier conclusion 'path-scoping cannot be expressed for the Write tool at all' was an over-generalization from an incomplete sweep. Working Write forms: Write(*) / bare Write, AND relative Write(<dir>/**). Notably the absolute $CASE_DIR/sub/** here is a no-op while S9's relative assets/** works -> the decisive factor looks like relative-path + doublestar. Consequence: dir-scoped nesting IS partially expressible via relative dir/** (but only as an ASK gate); single-star / absolute / exact-path forms are no-ops.
